turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: Justice Scalia, I don't think that the language of the statute is sufficiently clear. We have the statute which says that the prosecutor shall have 180 days after the prisoner shall have caused to be delivered this request. Now, you have three concepts. One is that the prisoner makes a demand. Two is that the demand is transmitted through his jailers, and three, that there's 180 days. And I think that from a grammatical standpoint or from a logical standpoint, the requirement of the 180 days will follow the other two requirements. So, the natural way that you would say this is that the prisoner makes a demand. He gives it to the jailer, and then there's 180 days that the prosecutor has to bring the action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I think it's a little different than that, isn't it, Mr. Payne? Supposing in the first of October, after the prosecutor had received the document, someone said to the prisoner on what date did you cause that document to be delivered to the prosecutor, what would your answer be? When did he cause it to be delivered?

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: He would -- if he knew what he was after, he would say it's September 7 when he signed it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, just say you're mailing a letter paying a bill, and after the bill has been paid somebody asked when did you cause that letter to be delivered. Would your answer be when it was received or when you mailed it?

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: I would say when it was mailed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course.

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: But I think that - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if you knew it never got there and somebody asked you the same question, when did you cause it to be delivered? You would still say on March 7, even though it was never delivered?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You'd say it was never delivered. Assume --

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: It's kind of an unusual phraseology.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But these have been delivered These are documents that have been delivered. That's undisputed, isn't it •p

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the only question is when did he cause it to be delivered.

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: He caused it on September 7, and - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Your argument would be a lot 'easier if the statute said sent, wouldn't it?

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: Yes, it would be, Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that a distinction we ought to bear in mind?

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: Yes, and it still is not clear, though, because normally you wouldn't ask somebody when did you cause your light bill to be paid. You would say when did you send it or when was it received.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you couldn't say when did you send it here because he doesn't send it, does he? He gives it to someone else to send it.

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: That's correct, and I would like to point out - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the statute doesn't say after it was delivered, does it? It says after he caused it to be delivered.

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: That's correct, and the emphasis should be on he, and it's on what he does.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Don't we still have the problem that he didn't cause it to be sent? The statute does not speak of his causing it to be sent. The statute speaks of his causing it to be delivered.

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's the distinction that I was trying to suggest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We'll resume there at 1:00, Mr. Payne.

turns.name: JOHN B. PAYNE, JR.
turns.text: Thank you.
